title: Managing a Python Installation
text: One of the great benefits of Python is its extensive network of packages. But with so many different packages comes interdependency and the difficulty of ensuring there are no conflicts with all the different possible configurations between package and Python versions.

title: Managing a Python Installation
text: One of the great benefits of Python is its extensive network of packages. But with so many different packages comes interdependency and the difficulty of ensuring there are no conflicts with all the different possible configurations between package and Python versions.

title: Managing a Python Installation
section: The Golden Rule of Python Environments
text:
The Golden Rule of Python Environments
When you want to install a package for Python (like pandas), your first instinct might be to open the terminal and type pip install pandas. Do not do this.
Operating systems like MacOS and Linux rely on a hidden, system-level Python installation to run core operating system background tasks. If you use global pip to install or upgrade packages, you risk overwriting the dependencies your operating system relies on. This leads to a broken OS, or at best, conflicting dependencies between your own projects (often called “Dependency Hell”).
Preventing Dependecy Hell: Every project must live in its own isolated “Virtual Environment.”
This is one of the downsides to Python, in order to prevent dependency conflicts you need to manage what amounts to different Python installations. Fortunately, this is no longer particularly tedious.

title: Managing a Python Installation
text:
uv
Historically, managing Python safely required juggling several different tools (pyenv for Python versions, venv for environments, pip for packages, and pipx for global apps).
There are multiple solutions to managing different Python installations (Conda/Miniconda are very popular), a recent one is uv. This replaces all of the traditional tools with a single tool. It operates entirely in user-space, meaning it requires no administrator privileges and generally can be installed without friction on computers that you do not have full control over.

1. Installing uv
Open your terminal and run the official standalone installer:
# On macOS/Linux
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

# On Windows
powershell -c "irm https://astral.sh/uv/install.ps1 | more"


2. Installing Python Versions
You do not need to download Python from the official website. uv will download and isolate whatever version of Python you need for a project.
For example, if you need a Python version of 3.12 as a requirement:
# Install the latest version of Python 3.12
uv python install 3.12


3. Creating a Project
When creating a new project, uv makes environment isolation automatic.
# Create a new project directory
mkdir data_analysis_project
cd data_analysis_project

# Initialize the project structure
uv init

# Add a package (this automatically creates the virtual environment!)
uv add pandas matplotlib
Notice that we never had to manually “activate” an environment. By using uv add, the tool automatically handles the creation and linkage of the isolated environment.


4. Running Your Code
To run a script using the isolated environment, simply prefix your command with uv run.
# Runs script.py using the isolated pandas/matplotlib installation
uv run script.py

title: Managing a Python Installation
text:
Installing Global Tools
Sometimes you need to install a Python application that you want to use everywhere, not just in one project. A good example is something like Jupyter Lab.
Installing an application into a specific project’s virtual environment is tedious, but installing them globally via pip breaks the Golden Rule.
The solution is an isolated tool runner.
# Install Jupyter Lab in its own isolated global environment
uv tool install jupyterlab

# You can now launch Jupyter from anywhere on your computer
jupyter lab
If you are working on a legacy system without uv, the traditional equivalent of this command is pipx install jupyterlab.

title: Managing a Python Installation
text:
Working with Older Codebases
While uv is the modern standard, you will inevitably encounter older codebases or tutorials that use the standard library tools. Adjusting them to use uv is straightforward.
If a repository contains a requirements.txt file, the traditional setup workflow looks like this:

Create the Environment:

python3 -m venv .venv

Activate the Environment: (You must do this every time you open a new terminal)

# macOS / Linux
source .venv/bin/activate

# Windows
.venv\Scripts\activate

Install the Dependencies:

pip install -r requirements.txt

Deactivate:

deactivate

title: Managing a Python Installation
text:
Recap
Need a library for a script? (e.g., numpy, pandas) -> Use uv add or a local venv.
Need a standalone app? (e.g., jupyter) -> Use uv tool install or pipx.
Never use: pip install [package] outside of an active virtual environment.

title: Course Overview
text: Welcome to the central repository for course materials, lecture notes, and computational resources. This site serves as the primary hub for our technical curriculum and will be updated weekly as we progress through the semester.

title: Course Overview
text:
Course Objectives
This course is designed to build a rigorous foundation in algorithmic thinking and scientific computing. By the end of the term, students will be expected to transition from basic scripting to developing structured, efficient solutions for engineering problems.
Our primary focus areas include: * Translating physical and mathematical models into computational logic. * Implementing and analyzing fundamental data structures. * Utilizing industry-standard tools for data analysis and visualization. * Developing robust debugging and code-testing practices.

title: Course Overview
text:
Semester Outline
The curriculum is divided into four primary modules. Select a module below to access the corresponding lecture notes, slide decks, and reading assignments.

Module 1: Foundations of Computation
Introduction to the computational environment, basic data types, control flow, and the principles of procedural programming.


Module 2: Data Structures and Algorithms
Moving beyond basic variables to explore lists, dictionaries, trees, and the algorithmic complexity of search and sort operations.


Module 3: Scientific Computing
Leveraging external libraries for matrix operations, applied linear algebra integrations, and numerical methods.


Module 4: Applied Engineering Projects
Synthesizing previous concepts to solve complex, open-ended engineering problems, including basic signal processing and data analysis.

title: Course Overview
text:
Core Resources
Access the essential platforms and documentation required for this course.

Syllabus and Policies - Grading rubrics, office hours, and academic integrity policies.
Assignment Submission Portal - Official university portal for submitting graded work.
In-Browser Python IDE - Development environment for practical exercises and examinations.

title: Course Overview
text:
Contact Information
For matters directly related to the coursework, please utilize the dedicated discussion boards to benefit the entire class. For individual administrative inquiries, refer to the contact protocols outlined on the main Faculty Profile.

title: Content Conventions
text:
The information in these course notes is categorized into three visual tiers that indicate the level of importance when it comes to graded material.

1. Core Foundations (Know Cold)

These blocks contain the core content of the course. If it is in a sharp-edged box, you are expected to be able to reproduce this information, derive the formulas, or perform the calculations from memory during examinations.




2. Reference Concepts (Functional Familiarity)

These blocks contain tools, syntax, or concepts that you should recognize and understand when you see them. You do not need to memorize these “cold,” but you should know they exist and where to find them.




3. Extended Mastery (Context & Depth)

These rounded blocks are for students looking to further explore related content. This material is not testable but provides may prove useful for mastery of the core material in the course.
